reforms.description: In 1998 and 1999, the government allocated significant financial resources to support small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in response to the economic crisis. This included W 3.8 trillion for job creation and W 2 trillion specifically aimed at easing the credit crunch, along with W 33 trillion in loan guarantees and additional budgetary support. A special task force was also established in banks to assist SMEs by automatically rolling over loans for priority firms.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The financial support and guarantees provided to SMEs are expected to enhance their stability and growth, thereby contributing positively to the economy.
reforms.status_evidence: The measures were actively put into place to support SMEs during the economic recovery phase.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reforms represent a significant structural change aimed at revitalizing the SME sector and supporting economic recovery.
reforms.source_quote: In 1998, an additional W 3.8 trillion was spent on job creation, including investment in social overhead capital and support for venture enterprises.

reforms.description: The Foreign Investment Promotion Act (FIPA) of November 1998 established a new legal framework for foreign direct investment in Korea, aimed at reducing restrictions and streamlining administrative procedures to attract foreign investors.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By attracting foreign investment, the reform is expected to enhance competition, innovation, and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The FIPA was enacted and has been in effect to facilitate foreign investment.
reforms.importance_rationale: The establishment of a new legal framework for foreign investment represents a significant overhaul of the investment landscape in Korea.
reforms.source_quote: The Foreign Investment Promotion Act (FIPA) of November 1998 changed the legal framework for foreign direct investment in Korea and explicitly established the principle of national treatment.
